summary: I grew up in the Philippines, raised by my mom and her sister, Tita Menchi, who I called Ma. My dad was away in the U.S. army, so my mom and aunt took care of me while my mom worked at a travel agency. Although my dad visited once a year, I often wished he was with us. I attended St. John's Academy there and experienced many important moments like my first communion and school events. Despite the distance, my mom kept our family connected through photos and letters.

My dad was stationed in the United States as part of the army, which meant he was away from us in the Philippines for most of my early life. In 1988, my mom and I got tourist visas and visited him in California, where I experienced Halloween and Christmas for the first time. Being together as a family, even for a short while, was very special because we knew our stay was limited. Though my dad continued to visit us annually, it took nine years before we were finally reunited. The immigration system still makes it hard for families like mine to stay together.

In 1988, my mom and I traveled to California to visit my dad, who was stationed there with the army. It was my first time experiencing American holidays like Halloween and Christmas, which was very exciting. Being in California allowed me to be with both of my parents, something I had longed for while growing up in the Philippines. Although our visit was temporary, it gave me a glimpse of family life together in the U.S. before we had to return home.

I attended St. John's Academy, a private Catholic school in the Philippines, where I was involved in many school events. I had my first communion there and received honors and recognition during yearly ceremonies. This school was an important part of my childhood, providing structure and community while my dad was away in the U.S. army. Even though my mom sent photos and letters, being at St. John's made me feel connected to my family and my faith.
